company_persona: a legal-tech document drafting assistant for law firms
title: The 85% cost creep
prompt: You're the PM for Redline, an AI drafting assistant used by law firms. Your CFO flags that inference spend per active user has risen 85% over the last six weeks, while active users grew only 5%. Nobody changed the pricing plan. Figure out what's happening and what you'd do about it.

hidden_facts.pattern: The rise is gradual and compounding over six weeks — not a step change on any single day.
hidden_facts.decomposition: Cost per user = calls per user × tokens per call × price per token. Price per token: unchanged. Calls per user: +12%. Tokens per call: +65%, almost entirely input tokens; output tokens per call are flat.
hidden_facts.flow_segment: The growth is concentrated in the 'review my draft' flow. Other flows are flat.
hidden_facts.feature_change: Seven weeks ago, a feature shipped that attaches the full document history of a matter (average ~40 pages) as context to every review call. As matters progress, that history grows — which is why cost climbs gradually.
hidden_facts.tenant_outlier: One enterprise tenant's misconfigured proxy causes timeout-retries, double-billing ~3% of total spend. Real but minor.
hidden_facts.caching_miss: The model provider offers prompt-caching discounts, but the payload assembles context in a different order each call, so the cache never hits. Fixing the ordering is estimated to cut input cost by ~55% on repeat calls.
hidden_facts.value_evidence: Users of full-history review convert to paid 30% better than others — the feature works; it's the implementation that's wasteful.
hidden_facts.retrieval_test: An internal eval showed retrieving the top-k relevant chunks of history instead of attaching everything scores within 2 points of full-history quality on the review eval.
hidden_facts.model_version: No model swap or provider price change occurred in the window.
hidden_facts.budget_ask: The CFO wants cost per active user back within 20% of the old baseline within a quarter.

ideal_answer_notes: Define the metric first: inference cost per active user, then decompose it mechanically — calls/user × tokens/call × price/token — before any hypothesis. The decomposition immediately localizes the problem to input tokens per call.
Characterize the pattern: gradual and compounding rules out a price change or a single bad deploy; something is growing over time. Segment by flow to find 'review my draft' carrying the growth.

Run the internal/external MECE: provider price change (no), model swap (no), traffic mix (minor, +12% calls), feature change (the 7-week-old full-history context — timing matches, and its growth-over-time signature matches the gradual curve), abuse/retries (the 3% tenant issue — real, minor).
Confirm rather than conclude: token histograms per flow over time, cost by tenant to catch the outlier, cache hit-rate stats. The compounding shape plus feature timing is the confirmation.

Fix without killing value: the feature converts 30% better — a naive rollback destroys value. The eval already shows top-k retrieval matches quality within 2 points; combine with cache-friendly payload ordering (~55% input savings) to meet the CFO's target while keeping the feature.
Sequence by effort and certainty: tenant retry fix (days), payload ordering for cache hits (a week, no quality risk), retrieval-based context behind the eval plus an A/B (weeks).
Install guardrails so it never recurs: per-flow cost-per-call budgets in launch review, token-anomaly alerts, cache-hit-rate dashboards, per-tenant spend caps.

Show AI cost literacy: input vs output token pricing, prompt caching mechanics, and context discipline are product decisions, not just engineering details — the case rewards knowing where LLM money actually goes.
Do the CFO math at the end: retries (−3%) + cache ordering (−~35% of total) + top-k context (large input reduction) plausibly returns to within 20% of baseline — state the plan against the target.
extra_checklist: Decomposes unit cost into calls × tokens × price and follows the dominant factor instead of guessing at causes
Preserves the high-converting feature by fixing context waste (retrieval, caching) rather than rolling it back
Installs cost guardrails — per-flow budgets, token anomaly alerts, cache-hit metrics — as standing launch gates

anchors.strong: Decomposes the metric before hypothesizing, reads the gradual pattern as something that grows, isolates input-token growth in the review flow, ties it to the full-history feature by timing and shape, catches the retry outlier, and lands a sequenced fix — cache ordering plus top-k retrieval validated by the existing eval — with the arithmetic tied back to the CFO's 20% target and standing guardrails.
anchors.at_bar: Structures internal vs external causes, finds the feature link with a couple of nudges, and proposes reducing context size, but doesn't decompose into the three cost factors unprompted, misses the caching angle or the retry outlier, and gives a fix without checking it against the quality eval or the CFO's numeric target.
anchors.weak: Guesses immediately ('the provider raised prices', 'users are using it more'), never asks for the cost decomposition or flow segmentation, proposes 'switch to a cheaper model' as the entire answer, and suggests rolling the feature back without asking whether it drives value.
